mappings.rationaleAI: Directly covers the periodic law, atomic number, electron configuration, and the systematic classification of elements (A and B).
Traces the discovery, properties, isotopes, and cosmic origins of the chemical elements, spanning element groups (B) and origin/abundances (D).
Introduces the periodic table, inorganic chemistry, spectroscopy, and the chemical properties distinguishing element groups (A and B).
Traces the arc from alchemy through Mendeleev's periodic table to modern elemental discovery, illuminating how chemical classification evolved (A and B).
Explains electron configuration, quantum mechanics of atomic structure, states of matter, and thermodynamic properties that govern element behavior (A and B).
Atomic structure and nuclear properties underpin the periodic table, and this volume connects radioactivity to C.2 (radioactive elements) and stellar nucleosynthesis to D.3 (elements in stars).
Isotopic composition and abundance shape the behavior of elements from hydrogen (B.1) through the actinides (B.13), with direct relevance to C.2 (stable and radioactive elements).
Radioactive decay, nuclear fission, and radioisotope applications illuminate C.2 (stable and radioactive elements) and C.5 (synthetic/transuranium elements).
Water's molecular structure and anomalous physical properties make it directly relevant to B.1 (hydrogen and water), the most abundant compound of the lightest element.
Chemical bonds, covalent bonding, and molecular structures reveal how elements combine, essential for understanding principal compounds across element groups (B).
Metals, ceramics, and their bonding and crystallographic properties connect to C.1 (metals vs. nonmetals) and C.7 (technologically significant elements).
Natural mineral forms—crystallography, solid solutions, ore deposits—illustrate how elements occur in Earth's crust (C.3, native and combined elements; D.1.a).
Life depends on specific elements—iron in hemoglobin, zinc in enzymes, phosphorus in DNA—making this essential for C.6 (biologically active or essential elements).
Nuclear fission of uranium and plutonium and fusion of hydrogen isotopes connect directly to B.13 (actinide elements) and C.5 (synthetic/transuranium elements).
Stellar nucleosynthesis—hydrogen burning, helium fusion, and the formation of heavier elements—is the origin story told in D.3 (elements in the stars).
Solar physics reveals the Sun's elemental composition, directly relevant to D.2 (elements in the solar system) and D.3 (elements in stars).
Big Bang nucleosynthesis produced the primordial hydrogen and helium from which all heavier elements descend, central to D (origin of the elements and their relative abundances).
Earth's chemical composition, radioactive decay, and geological differentiation govern element distribution across the planet, relevant to D.1 (elements on Earth).
Earth's internal structure and physical properties determine how elements are distributed between crust and mantle, relevant to D.1.a (elements in the crust).
Atmospheric composition—nitrogen, oxygen, argon, carbon dioxide—is a direct expression of D.1.c (elements in the atmosphere).
Biogeochemical cycling moves elements through the biosphere, relevant to D.1.d (elements in the biosphere) and the global cycling of carbon, nitrogen, and phosphorus.
Planetary composition reflects the solar nebula from which planets condensed, directly relevant to D.2 (elements in the solar system).
